6-science-earth-space-plate-tectonics.prompt: Which observation best supports the idea that continents now separated by oceans were once connected?

options.text: Matching fossils and rock layers appear on both sides of the Atlantic Ocean.
options.feedback: Matching fossils and rock patterns are strong evidence that the continents were once joined.

options.text: Different continents have different weather today.
options.feedback: Different weather does not show that continents used to touch.

options.text: All continents have beaches somewhere along their coasts.
options.feedback: Beaches do not explain past plate motion.

options.text: Some continents are larger than others.
options.feedback: Size differences are not evidence that continents moved.

6-science-earth-space-plate-tectonics.prompt: Why do many earthquakes and volcanoes happen in long belts instead of being spread randomly across Earth?

options.text: They usually form near plate boundaries where plates interact.
options.feedback: Plate interactions concentrate earthquakes and volcanoes near boundaries.

options.text: They only happen where the weather changes quickly.
options.feedback: Weather does not control tectonic patterns.

options.text: They happen only on islands in the Pacific Ocean.
options.feedback: Many occur around the Pacific, but they also happen in other boundary zones.

options.text: They form wherever Earth's crust is oldest.
options.feedback: Age alone does not explain the narrow boundary pattern.

6-science-earth-space-plate-tectonics.prompt: Which ocean-floor pattern is strongest evidence for seafloor spreading at a mid-ocean ridge?

options.text: Rocks closest to the ridge are youngest, and rocks get older farther away on both sides.
options.feedback: That age pattern fits new crust forming at the ridge and moving outward.

options.text: The deepest parts of the ocean are always next to the ridge.
options.feedback: Mid-ocean ridges are elevated, not the deepest parts.

options.text: All ocean-floor rocks have the same age.
options.feedback: If all rocks were the same age, it would not support spreading.

options.text: No earthquakes happen near the ridge.
options.feedback: Earthquakes can occur near spreading centers.

6-science-earth-space-plate-tectonics.prompt: At a convergent boundary where an oceanic plate meets a continental plate, what usually happens?

options.text: The denser oceanic plate sinks beneath the continental plate.
options.feedback: Oceanic crust is usually denser, so it subducts beneath continental crust.

options.text: The plates move apart and form a ridge.
The plates slide past each other without changing elevation.

options.feedback: Plate boundaries are active because plates continue to move.

6-science-earth-space-plate-tectonics.prompt: Which landform is most strongly linked to a divergent plate boundary?

options.text: A mid-ocean ridge where new crust forms
options.feedback: Divergent boundaries create ridges and rift zones as plates move apart.

options.text: A deep trench beside a continent
options.feedback: Deep trenches usually form at convergent subduction zones.

options.text: A chain of folded mountains from plate collision
options.feedback: Folded mountain ranges are more closely tied to convergent motion.

options.text: A fault where plates grind sideways past each other
options.feedback: Sideways motion is a transform pattern.

6-science-earth-space-plate-tectonics.prompt: What hazard is most common along a transform boundary?

options.text: Earthquakes caused by built-up stress being released
options.feedback: Transform boundaries often produce earthquakes as plates lock and then slip.

options.text: New crust forming at a ridge
A wide line of volcanic islands from magma rising through a subducting slab
options.feedback: Volcanic arcs are more typical of convergent subduction zones.

options.text: No major geologic activity at all
options.feedback: Transform boundaries are active and can produce powerful earthquakes.

6-science-earth-space-plate-tectonics.prompt: A student says matching coastlines alone prove that plates move. Which added evidence would make the explanation much stronger?

options.text: Matching fossils and mountain belts on continents now separated by oceans
options.feedback: Multiple lines of related evidence make the continental-connection claim much stronger.

options.text: A list of countries located on each continent today
options.feedback: Modern country borders do not support plate-tectonics reasoning.

options.text: A photo showing that some coastlines have beaches
options.feedback: Beaches do not explain why continents may once have fit together.

options.text: A weather report from both continents on the same day
options.feedback: Weather is short-term and unrelated to ancient plate motion.

6-science-earth-space-plate-tectonics.prompt: Scientists observe a deep trench offshore, frequent earthquakes, and a chain of volcanoes along a continent. Which boundary best fits that evidence?

options.text: A convergent boundary where an oceanic plate subducts beneath a continental plate
options.feedback: That combination of trench, quakes, and volcanoes is classic subduction-zone evidence.

options.text: A divergent boundary where plates are moving apart on the ocean floor
options.feedback: Divergent boundaries form ridges, not deep trenches plus volcanic arcs on a continent.

options.text: A transform boundary where plates only slide sideways
options.feedback: Transform boundaries often cause earthquakes but do not usually create trenches and volcanic arcs together.

options.text: A hotspot far from any plate boundary
options.feedback: A hotspot can make volcanoes, but it does not explain the trench and quake pattern.

6-science-earth-space-plate-tectonics.prompt: Why does the west coast of South America have both many earthquakes and the Andes volcanoes?

options.text: An oceanic plate is subducting beneath a continental plate, causing stress and melting.
options.feedback: Subduction explains both earthquake stress and volcano formation along the Andes.

options.text: A divergent boundary is pulling the continent apart and making a ridge on land.
options.feedback: That would not explain the trench-and-volcano arc pattern.

options.text: Transform motion creates volcanoes whenever plates slide sideways.
options.feedback: Transform motion commonly causes earthquakes, not large volcanic arcs.

options.text: Winds from the Pacific Ocean push the crust upward into mountains.
options.feedback: Surface winds do not drive tectonic plates.

6-science-earth-space-plate-tectonics.prompt: Which statement correctly connects evidence to an explanation about plate motion?

options.text: Because rocks near a mid-ocean ridge are youngest, scientists infer that new crust forms there and then moves outward.
options.feedback: That option uses evidence and then explains what the evidence means.

options.text: Because ridges are underwater, they must be unimportant to plate motion.
options.feedback: Being underwater does not make a feature unimportant.

options.text: Because continents have beaches, they must have once been joined.
options.feedback: That is not relevant evidence for continental motion.

options.text: Because earthquakes happen, plate boundaries can never be identified.
options.feedback: Earthquake patterns actually help scientists identify boundaries.

6-science-earth-space-plate-tectonics.prompt: Which claim is best supported by finding the same ancient plant fossils in South America and Africa?

options.text: The continents were once connected and later moved apart.
options.feedback: Matching land-fossil evidence supports the idea of past continental connection.

options.text: Plants can easily grow across a wide ocean without transport.
options.feedback: That is a much weaker explanation for matching fossils.

options.text: Fossils always form at transform boundaries.
options.feedback: Fossils are not limited to transform boundaries.

options.text: Continents never change position after they form.
options.feedback: The fossil evidence points in the opposite direction.

6-science-earth-space-plate-tectonics.prompt: A long crack valley is forming as a continent slowly pulls apart. Which boundary type best explains this pattern?

options.text: A divergent boundary forming a rift zone
options.feedback: Continental rifts form where plates pull apart.

options.text: A convergent boundary forming a trench
options.feedback: Convergent boundaries push together instead of pulling apart.

options.text: A transform boundary with only sideways motion
options.feedback: Sideways sliding does not usually create a widening rift valley.

options.text: A hotspot with no plate motion involved
options.feedback: A hotspot can create volcanoes, but it does not best explain a continent splitting apart.
